candidates.description: The prominent dark opening visible in image 8 (Northwest) is a drainage infrastructure element - a storm drain or gutter inlet with a missing cover. It has straight, constructed rectangular edges and is positioned exactly at the curb/gutter junction typical of drainage infrastructure. This is not a pothole or asphalt failure.

The road surface shows multiple patch repairs and utility covers (manholes) visible across the intersection. The large rectangular concrete sections in the roadway are utility access covers and prior patch repairs, not potholes. No actual holes with missing asphalt are visible.

The street view images show an urban area in Panama City (Obarrio neighborhood based on signage). The road surface appears to be asphalt in fair condition with some general wear. The large brown/gray areas visible in the lower portions of multiple images (particularly images 1, 7, 8, 9) are the characteristic near-nadir stitching artifacts from Google Street View, not actual road damage. No actual potholes with missing asphalt, exposed base material, or jagged edges are visible.

The apparent opening visible in images 1, 2, and 9 is a drainage infrastructure opening - a storm drain or culvert inlet located at the curb/sidewalk junction. It has straight, constructed concrete edges typical of drainage infrastructure, positioned exactly where storm water would collect at the gutter line. This is not an asphalt pothole but a built drainage feature, possibly with a missing or damaged cover.

The large dark area visible in multiple images is a near-nadir stitching artifact from Google Street View - a flat, textureless gray-brown smear without discernible pavement texture or edges. This artifact appears consistently across all cardinal direction views but shows no actual hole, depression, exposed base material, or jagged edges that would indicate real road damage.

The images show a commercial street in Panama City with parallel parking and retail frontage. The apparent dark area in the road is actually standing water/wet pavement from recent rain, not a pothole. Images show drainage grates along curbs (yellow-painted metal grates visible in images 1, 2, 3, 9), functioning storm drainage infrastructure.

The location shows a commercial/retail area with parking lots and street. A circular manhole cover is visible in the center of the roadway in images 4 and 5. The road surface appears to be in good condition with normal asphalt wear. Linear drainage grates/channels are visible along the parking area curb in images 1-3 and 9.

The location shows a commercial parking area with a prominent linear drainage channel/gutter running along the edge of the parking spaces. The drainage infrastructure has a gold/brass-colored metal grate cover and is properly installed. No asphalt potholes are visible in any of the 10 images.

The location shows a street intersection in what appears to be Panama City (based on coordinates and yellow taxi visible). The street view images show typical urban road surfaces with normal wear. Image 9 (Down view) shows the characteristic near-nadir stitching artifact - a flat, blurry gray/brown smear with no discernible pavement texture that is common in Google Street View imagery.
